title: Telegram Stays Blocked Ahead of NEET; Cough Syrups Now Require Prescription
summary: The Centre's Telegram block, imposed to prevent paper-leak rumours ahead of the June 21 NEET re-examination, remains in effect until June 22. In a separate but simultaneously reported regulatory move, the government amended the 1945 Drugs Rules to require a doctor's prescription for all cough syrup sales, ending over-the-counter availability nationwide.

coverages.headline: Telegram access curbed ahead of NEET; over-the-counter sale of cough syrups banned
coverages.excerpt: The Centre blocked Telegram until June 22 to prevent paper-leak claims around the NEET re-examination, and amended the 1945 Drugs Rules to require doctor's prescriptions for all syrups.
coverages.bias_notes: Bundling these two stories into a single roundup item obscures that they are unrelated regulatory actions on completely different timelines and justifications — the Telegram block is an acute, exam-specific measure with a fixed end date, while the cough syrup prescription rule is a permanent structural change to drug retail. Readers skimming the combined headline could easily miss that the syrup rule has no connection to NEET at all and will outlast the exam controversy by years. The cough syrup change is arguably the more consequential and underreported story here — it affects every household in India indefinitely, while the Telegram block affects a contained population for five days.
